---
title: "Additional concerns"
---

The issues gathered here are smaller in scope than those in the earlier chapters — think
of it as housekeeping. Four independent problems, each with a clear fix, none of which
threatens the dissertation's central argument. Sorting them out before submission makes
the manuscript sharper and forestalls the kind of reviewer queries that slow a review
without adding anything substantive.

## The condition count doesn't add up (a reproducibility flag)

**What happens.** Table 18 (p. 84) defines the Study 3 grid along four factors: sample
size (50, 100, 500 — three levels), data-collection design (complete, incomplete, anchor —
three), effect type (none, severity, misfit — three), and rater correlation (0.2, 0.6, 0.9
— three). That is 3 × 3 × 3 × 3 = 81 conditions. Yet the text says "we created **108
combinations** of simulation conditions" (p. 85), and the Discussion quietly introduces a
fourth sample size — "n = 50, 100, 500, and **900**" (p. 89) — that appears nowhere in the
methods or in Table 18. The arithmetic resolves immediately: 108 = 4 × 3 × 3 × 3, so the
only route to 108 is a fourth sample-size level, which is exactly the stray n = 900.

```{r}
#| label: cond-count
#| code-summary: "Show the arithmetic"
#| message: false
cat("Table 18 grid:  3 × 3 × 3 × 3 =", 3^4, "conditions\n")
cat("Stated in text:                 108 conditions\n")
cat("108 = 4 × 3 × 3 × 3 =", 4 * 3^3, " → needs a 4th sample size (the stray n = 900)\n\n")

# A "count" framing of the false-flag problem at the TWO-SIDED ±0.5-logit cut: how many
# NORMAL raters (true severity ~ N(0, 0.5)) are flagged by chance as too severe OR too
# lenient (|estimated λ| > 0.5, either direction)? This two-sided rate (~0.318) is ~2×
# Chapter 5's ONE-sided +0.5 false-positive rate (~0.159); the count below is R × 0.318.
fp <- data.frame(R = c(5, 10, 50))                      # raters in the dataset
fp$N    <- fp$R * 10                                    # examinees (raters = N/10)
fp$se   <- 1 / sqrt(fp$N * 5 * 0.77)                    # SE of a location (0.77 ≈ avg score variance W, cf. Ch.5/Ch.2)
fp$rate <- 2 * (1 - pnorm(0.5 / sqrt(0.5^2 + fp$se^2))) # P(|estimated λ| > 0.5)
fp$falsely_flagged <- round(fp$R * fp$rate, 1)
fp[, c("R", "rate", "falsely_flagged")]
```

At R = 50 raters, the table's analytic *expected count* is roughly **16** normal raters
tripping the ±0.5-logit flag by chance alone — where "flagged" means estimated *too severe
or too lenient*, i.e. |λ| > 0.5 in *either* direction (a two-sided count). This is the
legible "how-many-raters" companion to Chapter 5's false-positive *rate* — but the two are
not the same number wearing different clothes. Chapter 5's ≈ 16% is a *one-sided* rate (the
+0.5 tail of N(0, 0.5)), which on its own is ≈ **8** of 50 raters; counting flags in either
direction roughly doubles the per-rater rate to ≈ 0.32, giving the ≈ 16 here. The matching
"16"s are therefore a coincidence — Chapter 5's *16 percent* and this chapter's *16 raters*
are different quantities that happen to land on the same digits.

**Why it matters.** Study 3 is entirely a simulation, so the condition grid *is* the
empirical basis. Any inconsistency among what Table 18 lists, what the text claims, and
what the ANOVAs actually processed means the results cannot be fully reproduced from the
manuscript alone — readers cannot verify which cells fed the analyses, and the appendix
tables become hard to interpret without knowing whether n = 900 was run.

**Fix.** Correct the count (to 81 or 108, whichever was actually run); add n = 900 to Table
18 if those cells were generated, or remove the Discussion reference if they were not; and
release the condition log or simulation script so the grid is machine-readable and
independently verifiable.

## Two names for the Study 3 generating model

**What happens.** The Study 3 design states that ratings were generated from "a modified
version of the MFRM that allows us to generate certain types of rater effects" (p. 84),
written out as Equation (8) — a many-facet model with rater severity $\lambda_j$, **rater
discrimination $\alpha_j$**, item difficulty $\delta_i$, and thresholds $\tau_{ik}$. Yet a
few lines down on the *same page*, the local-independence factor is described as starting
from ratings simulated under a different model: "After simulating baseline ratings using the
**GPCM**, we randomly selected rater pairs…" (p. 84). The two phrases name two different
data-generating models. The GPCM (Muraki, 1992) is an *item-level* polytomous model — item
discrimination $a_i$, no rater facets — whereas the adapted MFRM of Equation (8) is a
*many-facet* model built around rater severity and rater discrimination. In the measurement
literature these are not interchangeable labels for one model.

**Why it matters.** Study 3 is a simulation, so the generating model *is* the data. As
written, a reader cannot tell whether the local-independence cells were built on the
Equation (8) many-facet model the rest of the chapter implies, or on a genuinely different
item-level GPCM — with the rater-pair dependence then injected on top of whichever baseline
was used. That ambiguity blocks exact reproduction of the very condition this study is named
for. It is almost certainly a wording slip rather than two real pipelines — the dissertation
uses the GPCM legitimately elsewhere as the HRM's first stage — but only a one-word
correction can settle it for the reader.

**Fix.** State the generating model once, consistently, and use that name throughout Study 3.
If (as is almost certain) Equation (8) is the intended baseline, replace "GPCM" on p. 84 with
the adapted-MFRM / Equation (8) reference; if a separate GPCM baseline really was used for the
local-independence factor, give its equation and say explicitly why it differs from Equation
(8). Either way, one named model end-to-end removes the ambiguity at no cost to the findings.

## Strengthening the ANOVA analysis

**What happens.** Each indicator is analyzed with a separate univariate ANOVA, and
practical significance is declared when partial η² ≥ .01, on data with 100 replications per
condition and multiple raters nested within each replication.

**Why it matters.** Three problems compound. First, raters within a replication share a
common data-generating context and are not independent; the ANOVA treats them as if they
were, understating standard errors. Second, the indicators are correlated outcomes analyzed
in parallel with no multivariate test or multiplicity adjustment, which inflates the
family-wise error rate invisibly. Third — and most consequential — with 100 replications ×
many raters, *p*-values are almost always significant, so the entire interpretation rests
on the η² ≥ .01 bar, which (as Chapter 5 showed) corresponds to near-chance detection. The
ANOVAs may be certifying "practically significant" factors whose practical contribution is
negligible.

**Fix.** Analyze replicate-level summaries (collapsing within each replication first), or
use a mixed-effects model with replication as a random factor — standard in simulation
methodology and familiar to reviewers. Pre-specify one or two primary outcomes (or apply a
multiplicity correction across the battery of ANOVAs), and report recovery/detection metrics
alongside — or instead of — the omnibus *F* and η², so effect sizes can be read on a scale
with direct practical meaning.

## Coding reliability of the systematic review

**What happens.** The systematic review that opens the dissertation was coded by a single
person: "I coded all articles based on the following criteria…" (p. 13), with
disagreements "resolved through discussion with my Dissertation Chair until a consensus was
reached" (pp. 13–14). No inter-coder reliability statistic — no Cohen's κ, no percent
agreement — is reported.

**Why it matters.** The systematic review's contribution is its frequency tables characterizing the
rater-effects literature, so their credibility rests on the coding. Let me be explicit that
this is strictly **additive, not fatal**: the review is a genuine, keepable contribution —
real work and real scholarship. But journal reviewers in measurement and applied
linguistics routinely expect either a second independent coder with a reported reliability
statistic on a sample of articles, or a documented coding audit; the current description
gives no basis for assessing how consistently the criteria were applied.

**Fix.** Double-code a random ~20% sample of the articles independently, compute κ and
percent agreement for each major category, and report them in the methods. If that is not
feasible before submission, a transparent coding audit — a supplementary codebook
documenting decision rules and borderline cases — will satisfy most reviewers.

::: {.callout-tip}
## Fix-it
1. **Condition count.** Reconcile the stated 108 with Table 18's 81-cell grid; add n = 900
   to Table 18 if those cells were run (or remove the reference); release the condition log.
2. **Study 3 generating model.** State one model end-to-end: p. 84 names both the Equation (8)
   adapted MFRM *and* the GPCM as the baseline that produced the ratings. If Equation (8) is
   intended (almost certainly), drop the stray "GPCM"; otherwise give the GPCM baseline its
   own equation and rationale.
3. **ANOVA hygiene.** Analyze replicate-level summaries (or mixed-effects models) to respect
   within-replication clustering; pre-specify primary outcomes or correct for multiplicity;
   report recovery/detection rates alongside *F* and η².
4. **Coding reliability.** Double-code ~20% of the systematic-review articles and report Cohen's κ /
   percent agreement, or supply a supplementary codebook documenting the decision rules.

And one reporting standard ties the simulation work together: attach a **Monte-Carlo
standard error** to every number both studies average over the 100 replications — Study 2's
and Study 3's condition means, detection/recovery rates, and AUC alike (the across-replication
SD over $\sqrt{100}$ for a mean, the binomial MC-SE for a rate), reported beside the quantity
in the tables — so a reader can separate a real between-condition difference from simulation
noise rather than reading 100-run averages as exact. It is the replication-level companion to
the posterior MCSE Chapter 4 asks for at the estimation level, and generalizes the MC-SE
Chapter 5 attaches to its detection metrics.
:::

All four are fixable in an afternoon — none changes the dissertation's central findings;
each makes the manuscript meaningfully more defensible to a skeptical reviewer.
